meta.source: Aggregated from 4 deliberation panels, 24 anonymous participants, 144 debate turns

case_summary: Public dispute between Luo Yonghao and Xibei founder Jia Guolong over pre-made food (预制菜) in restaurants. Luo Yonghao criticized chain restaurants for using pre-made food while presenting it as freshly cooked. Xibei closed 102 stores. Both sides exchanged accusations of misinformation and harm.
core_consensus_across_all_panels: Consumers have a legitimate right to know how their food is prepared
Current transparency in chain restaurants is insufficient -- open kitchens show only the final step, not central kitchen pre-processing
Mandatory labeling of pre-processed food in restaurants is the most practical solution
Frontline workers (servers, chefs) are the primary collateral damage of elite conflicts
Doxxing children and abusing service workers is categorically unacceptable regardless of the dispute's merits
Institutional solutions (labeling laws, enforcement mechanisms) are needed -- individual goodwill is insufficient

key_proposals_with_broad_support.description: Label each dish as 'fresh-made' / 'partially pre-processed' / 'mainly pre-processed' with simple icons
key_proposals_with_broad_support.support_basis: National policy already calls for 'vigorous promotion of disclosure'; has international precedent; simple and actionable
key_proposals_with_broad_support.suggested_implementation: Pilot in major cities, collect feedback, optimize, then nationwide rollout

key_proposals_with_broad_support.proposal: Four-layer governance for public opinion supervision
key_proposals_with_broad_support.description: Information transparency (foundation) -> Public opinion as alarm (supplement) -> Independent fact-checking (intermediary) -> Institutional enforcement (guarantee)
key_proposals_with_broad_support.support_basis: Prevents both unchecked influencer power and institutional silence on genuine problems
key_proposals_with_broad_support.suggested_implementation: Phased implementation starting with mandatory food information disclosure

key_proposals_with_broad_support.description: Reduce amplification of extreme content, especially content involving doxxing or real-world violence
key_proposals_with_broad_support.support_basis: Extreme content receives disproportionate algorithmic promotion, creating positive feedback loops of escalation
key_proposals_with_broad_support.suggested_implementation: Human review pre-checks for sensitive content, reduced recommendation weight for extreme material

areas_of_disagreement.topic: Whether Luo Yonghao's criticism was justified in method
areas_of_disagreement.range: From 'necessary alarm that exposed real problems' to 'irresponsible amplification that harmed innocent workers'
areas_of_disagreement.nuance: Most participants acknowledged both: the problems exposed were real, but the methods caused disproportionate collateral damage

areas_of_disagreement.topic: Whether pre-made food is inherently problematic
areas_of_disagreement.range: From 'deceptive when undisclosed' to 'convenient and quality-acceptable'
areas_of_disagreement.nuance: No participant argued pre-made food should be banned; disagreement was about transparency and fair pricing

moral_red_lines_no_participant_crossed: Physical abuse of service workers is never acceptable
Consumers deserve to know what they are paying for
Frontline workers should not bear punishment for management decisions

participant_demographics_represented.occupation_range: Construction worker, delivery rider, chef, supermarket cashier, full-time mother, restaurant server, office worker, MCN operator, food blogger, college student, retired factory worker, rural farmer, middle school student
participant_demographics_represented.stakeholder_positions: Brand loyalist, hostile former employee, professional critic, industry insider, indifferent bystander, analytical skeptic

what_changed_minds: Citation of specific policy text: When the six-ministry notice's disclosure requirement was quoted verbatim, even brand defenders conceded selective compliance was the real problem
Firsthand testimony: Former employee accounts of workplace abuse shifted abstract policy debates into concrete human impact

Interest distribution analysis: When a chef pointed out that cost savings from central kitchens were kept by companies while prices stayed the same, consumer anger was reframed from 'feeling deceived' to 'being overcharged'

Cross-demographic moral consensus: When a rural grandmother, a 14-year-old girl, and an MCN operator all agreed that harming children is unacceptable, it demonstrated that moral red lines transcend all other divisions
